content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 2, 1992

Glen H. Spain
Attorney at Law
P.O. Box 205
Comptche, CA 95427

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-534

Dear Mr. Spain:

You have requested advice on behalf of Forests Forever, Inc.
(the "Corporation") and its sponsored ballot measure committee,
Forests Forever November '92 (the "Committee"), regarding the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act's definition of "contribution" include
legal services provided by you to the Corporation and the
Committee and related miscellaneous expenses if payment for the
services and expenses will be deferred until sufficient funds have
been raised?

2. If the answer to Question 1 is yes, are such
contributions disclosed as "in-kind" loans?

3. If the Corporation makes a repayment on an in-kind loan
on behalf of the Committee, is the repayment a contribution?

4. Does the answer to Question 3 differ if both the
Corporation and the Committee are jointly liable for repayment of
the in-kind loan?

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Glen H. Spain
No. A-91-534
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1.  Legal services provided to and related expenses incurred
on behalf of the Committee for which payment will be made at a
later date are contributions if the Committee has begun
circulation of the signature petitions to qualify the measure for
the ballot.  Legal expenses related to drafting the petition and
qualifying the measure for the ballot which were incurred prior to
the time signature petitions are circulated, are neither
reportable contributions nor expenditures.

2.  Yes.  A contribution in the form of goods or services
for which payment will be made at a later date must be disclosed
as an "in-kind" loan.

3.  The Corporation's repayment of an in-kind loan on behalf
of the Committee is a contribution.  The reporting of such
contributions is discussed in the Analysis below.

4.  The answer to Question 3 does not change if the
Corporation and the Committee are jointly liable for repayment of
the loan.

FACTS

You are the general counsel for Forests Forever, Inc., a
nonprofit political action organization.  One of the primary
purposes of this organization is to draft statewide ballot
initiatives and to organize and manage campaigns for those
statewide ballot initiatives it has drafted.  You recently drafted
a statewide ballot measure and submitted it for filing.  In
addition, you recently formed a recipient committee entitled
"Forests Forever November '92 Committee."  The Committee is a
ballot measure committee sponsored by Forests Forever, Inc.

Your services, prior to the formation of the Committee,
included drafting the ballot initiative and processing the
required legal documents through the appropriate governmental
agencies for filing with the Secretary of State.  These services
were considered to be joint obligations of the Corporation and the
Committee and payable by either.

Future services you will provide to the Committee include
reviewing its reports and filings, providing legal advice with
respect to campaign issues, and litigation work which may arise
during the course of the campaign.  All of these services will be
billable to the Committee but with payment guaranteed by the
Corporation if the Committee's funds are insufficient to pay the
obligations.

Glen H. Spain
No. A-91-534
Page 3


ANALYSIS

A "contribution" is a payment made for political purposes.
(Section 82015.) Regulation 18215 further defines "contribution,"
in pertinent part, as follows:

     (a) A contribution is any monetary or
nonmonetary payment made for political purposes for
which full and adequate consideration is not made
to the donor. A payment is made for political
purposes if it is:

...

     (2) Received by or made at the behest of:

...

          (D) An organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes....

     Goods and services provided to the Committee for which full
and adequate consideration is not paid within the normal course of
business are contributions. (Section 82015.) Such contributions
are disclosed as "in-kind" loans if payment for the goods or
services will be made at a later date. A contribution in the form
of an in-kind loan is disclosed on Schedule C of the Form 419
(Ballot Measure Committee Campaign Statement) filed for the period
in which the services were provided. If a payment is made on the
loan during that reporting period, the payment is disclosed on
Schedule E. If payment is not made during the reporting period,
disclose the in-kind loan as an accrued expense on Schedule F.

     Certain payments incurred in connection with qualifying a
measure for the ballot are not contributions or reportable
campaign expenditures under the Act. (Sections 82015 and 82025.)
In the Fontana Opinion (1976, 2 FPPC Ops. 25),3/ the Commission
determined that the expenditures for legal expenses incurred prior to
the time petitions are circulated to qualify a measure for the
ballot are neither expenditures nor contributions. (Beatty Advice
Memo, No. T-87-092.)


3/     Copies of Commission regulations and opinions are available
in many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials
and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission
at a cost of 10¢ per page.

Glen H. Spain
No. A-91-534
Page 4


     All payments made at the behest of the Committee are
contributions, including loan repayments made by the Corporation
on the Committee's behalf.  (Section 82015, Regulation 1825,
supra, and Seeley Advice Letter, No. A-86-269.)  This advice does
not change if the Committee and the Corporation are jointly
responsible for payment of a debt.  Therefore, any debt for which
the Committee is either fully or jointly responsible must be
disclosed on the Committee's campaign disclosure statement.

     In any reporting period in which the Corporation makes a
payment on behalf of the Committee, the Committee must disclose
the Corporation's payment as a monetary contribution on Schedule A
and as an expenditure on Schedule E, or a payment of an accrued
expense on Schedule F, whichever is applicable.  A notation should
be made on the campaign statement indicating that the expenditure
was made from the Corporation's account.

     Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 if you
have additional questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Lynda Cassady
                                             Political Reform Consultant
